nativeRange.habitat: Swampy parts of coastal rainforest — slow-flowing brooks, raphia swamps, and temporary pools within the Niger Delta lowlands; prefers shallow, heavily vegetated, dimly lit water with soft leaf-litter substrate

decorPreferences: dark substrate (peat, fine sand, or dark gravel)
dense planting (Java fern, Anubias, water sprite, floating plants)
driftwood and leaf litter for cover and tannin release
caves or dense vegetation as visual barriers between males

diet.notes: Primarily live or frozen foods: bloodworms, daphnia, brine shrimp, white worms, black worms, chopped earthworms, and small crustaceans. Will occasionally accept high-quality flake or pellet foods, but dried foods alone are insufficient for long-term health and optimal coloration. Feed twice daily in small portions; a high-protein varied diet is essential to maintain condition and breeding readiness.

goodWith: peaceful cichlids of similar size (e.g., Apistogramma kept in separate territories)
larger characins too big to be eaten
robust similarly sized West African species
avoidWith: small fish that can be eaten (guppies, tetras, small rasboras)
other male Blue Gularis (extreme conspecific aggression)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Fundulopanchax sjostedti. Verify availability and license before use.

summary: West African non-annual killifish reaching 14 cm; the largest Fundulopanchax. Native to Niger Delta swamps of Nigeria and Cameroon. Requires soft, acidic water, a live/frozen-food diet, and a tight-fitting lid — an accomplished jumper. Males are highly conspecifically aggressive. fw-fish-278 and fw-fish-279 are merge candidates (sjoestedti vs sjostedti spelling variants of the same species).
careNotes:
The Blue Gularis (Fundulopanchax sjoestedti) is the largest killifish in its genus, with males reaching up to 14 cm and displaying spectacular blue, red, and yellow patterning. Unlike the majority of popular killifish, it is not an annual species — it does not inhabit temporary pools that dry seasonally and does not require a desiccation period for eggs; it is long-lived (3–5 years in captivity) and continuous spawning under appropriate conditions.

A tight-fitting lid is non-negotiable: this species is an accomplished jumper and will exploit any gap. A minimum 80-litre tank (90 cm footprint) suits a single pair; larger tanks allow the recommended 1 male : 2 female grouping. Males are highly aggressive toward each other and must not be housed together without very large tanks with substantial visual barriers. Males can also harass females; a ratio of one male to two females, with dense planting for refuge, reduces injury risk.

Water chemistry should be soft and slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 2–12, kH 1–5), mimicking the tannin-stained forest pools of the Niger Delta. Dark substrates and floating plants reduce light levels and stress. Peat moss or Indian Almond leaves help acidify the water and create a more natural environment.

Diet must be predominantly live or frozen animal prey. Bloodworms, daphnia, brine shrimp, white worms, and black worms are staples. Prepared foods are accepted occasionally but cannot form the core diet; reliance on dried food leads to nutritional deficiency and color loss. Feed twice daily in small amounts.

Conservation note: Fundulopanchax sjostedti is listed as Endangered (EN) by the IUCN, due to habitat loss across its restricted Niger Delta range.

Taxonomic note: fw-fish-278 (blue-gularis) and fw-fish-279 (blue-gularis-killifish) are merge candidates. Both represent Fundulopanchax sjostedti (Lönnberg, 1895). The original 1895 description used the spelling "sjoestedti" (reflecting German umlaut-expansion convention for Sjöstedt). The Catalog of Fishes (Eschmeyer/CAS) subsequently standardized the name to "sjostedti" on the basis that the honoree\'s name (Bror Yngve Sjöstedt) is Swedish rather than German, making the oe-expansion non-standard. Both spellings appear widely in the hobby and specialist literature; "sjostedti" is the currently accepted form per Catalog of Fishes, while "sjoestedti" is the original Lönnberg orthography retained by several European killifish registries and SeriouslyFish. See also: Aphyosemion sjoestedti (former genus placement, widely used in pre-2000 literature); Fundulopanchax zimmeri and Aphyosemion caeruleum (additional synonyms).
breedingNotes:
The Blue Gularis is a substrate spawner — not an annual species — and does not require egg desiccation, though eggs can survive a short dry period. Breeding is moderately difficult due to egg management challenges and male aggression.

A dedicated breeding tank (minimum 15" × 8" × 8") is recommended: soft water (pH 6.5), temperature 23–25°C, and a substrate of peat moss or a spawning mop over fine substrate. One male to two females is the standard ratio; males can be very aggressive toward females and subordinate individuals. Provide dense planting and visual barriers.

Females deposit eggs continuously, potentially 50–60 eggs per week over 2–3 weeks. Eggs are adhesive and deposited among substrate and plant roots. Eggs hatch in approximately 3–5 weeks at 23°C; fungal infection of eggs is a common failure point — slight salinity, methylene blue, or careful egg separation is recommended. Fry accept brine shrimp nauplii, micro-worms, and sieved daphnia from hatching. Raise fry separately as adults will eat them.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Fundulopanchax sjoestedti
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (12 cm SL), temperature (23–26°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (5–20°H), minimum tank (90×30×30 cm, 80 L), diet (live/frozen small foods), compatibility guidance, breeding setup and egg incubation (~21 days), and habitat (coastal rainforest swamps, Nigeria and Cameroon). Family listed as Aplocheilidae on this page — cross-checked as Nothobranchiidae per FishBase and Wikipedia (Aplocheilidae and Nothobranchiidae are sometimes treated synonymously or as subfamilies in older classifications; Nothobranchiidae used here as the current consensus family per FishBase and Catalog of Fishes).

additional.name: FishBase — Fundulopanchax sjostedti
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (up to 14 cm TL), temperature (23–26°C), native range (Niger Delta, southern/southeastern Nigeria and southwestern Cameroon), family (Nothobranchiidae), order (Cyprinodontiformes), habitat (temporary swamps, raphia-swamps, slow-flowing brooks in coastal rainforest), conservation status (Endangered EN). Uses spelling 'sjostedti' per Catalog of Fishes standard.

additional.name: Eschmeyer's Catalog of Fishes — Fundulopanchax sjostedti
additional.notes: Authoritative taxonomic source. Confirms current valid name as Fundulopanchax sjostedti (Lönnberg, 1895) with note that 'sjostedti' is the correct standardized spelling rather than 'sjoestedti', because the honoree's name is Swedish (Sjöstedt) not German — oe-expansion of Swedish ö is non-standard. Type locality: Ndian River waterfall (4°57'N 8°53'E) and Bonge, western Cameroon. Syntypes at ZMUU 272. Lists Aphyosemion sjoestedti and Fundulus sjostedti as synonyms.

additional.name: British Killifish Association — Fundulopanchax sjoestedti
additional.notes: Specialist killifish society profile. Confirmed male size ~5 inches (12–13 cm), temperature 70–74°F (21–23°C, cooler preference for longevity), pH 6.5, hardness 50–80 ppm (~3–5 dGH), breeding method (bottom spawner, trio 1M:2F), egg production (50–60/week for 2–3 weeks), incubation ~5 weeks, fry diet (BBS, microworms, sieved daphnia). Notes males 'very quarrelsome', females sometimes too; fish must be 'equal proportions' to avoid injury.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Fundulopanchax sjostedti
additional.notes: General reference. Confirmed taxonomy (Nothobranchiidae, Cyprinodontiformes), native range (Nigeria and Cameroon, Niger Delta), male size up to 14 cm, common names (blue gularis, golden pheasant gularis, red aphyosemion), spelling etymology (named for Swedish naturalist Bror Yngve Sjöstedt, 1866–1948), synonyms (Aphyosemion sjostedti, Aphyosemion caeruleum, Fundulopanchax zimmeri). Conservation status: Endangered (IUCN).

additional.name: aqua-fish.net — Blue Gularis (Fundulopanchax sjostedti)
additional.notes: Supplementary aquarium trade source. Provided size (11–13 cm), temperature (22–26°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness (4–18°N / ~71–321 ppm), minimum tank (75 L for single specimen), feeding frequency (twice daily, small portions), lifespan (up to 3 years cited; cross-checked against other sources yielding 3–5 year range used). Native range listed incorrectly as 'Sierra Leone and Western Liberia' — disregarded; FishBase/SeriouslyFish/Wikipedia consensus of Nigeria/Cameroon used.

fish.breedingNotes: Substrate spawner (non-annual); continuous egg layer producing 50–60 eggs per week under breeding conditions. Eggs prone to fungal infection — methylene blue or careful isolation recommended. Incubation ~3–5 weeks at 23°C. Eggs can tolerate short dry storage but desiccation is not required. Fry accept BBS and microworms from hatching. Ratio: 1 male : 2 females; raise fry separately.
